hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Message: 'Ummm no. I want you to review the ENTIRE session… thats the point of retro.' Core entities: [retro, session, ENTIRE]. Output requirement: full-session sweep, no filtering. Constraint: 'the point of retro' signals the scope is definitional, not negotiable. What is provided: correction to a prior partial output. What is missing: no specification of what was omitted — only that something was.
hypotheses.diagnose: The gap is scope truncation. Brain produced a retro that covered less than the full session. The ambiguity is only in degree — 'ENTIRE' admits no wiggle room. No clarity gap on intent; the message is unambiguous. Blocking risk: if retro re-runs but still filters by perceived salience, the correction will repeat.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: corrective procedural. Technique: constraint-based + precision focus. Role: session auditor with zero editorial license. The retro skill must sweep every exchange in the session — not just the high-signal ones, not just the ones that produced rules, not just the failures. Every exchange is a candidate for observation, correction, or confirmation. The prior run apparently applied a salience filter that Brain chose unilaterally.
hypotheses.deliver: Re-invoke /brain-retro with explicit scope: review every exchange in this session from message 1 to present, in order. Do not pre-filter by perceived importance. Every correction, every clarification, every direction-change, every tool call that was challenged — all are candidates for hippocampus entries or prefrontal rules. Produce the batch, present it to Justs, then commit after corrections.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Identity layer flags this exact pattern explicitly: 'when Justs says he wants the full thing reviewed he means the full thing — not a filtered subset shaped by what I think he meant.' The active-identity situational block also names the fabrication reflex and the permission-asking pattern as live risks. The 'Ummm no' opener signals Justs caught Brain scoping down without permission.
hypotheses.diagnose: Brain applied an editorial filter to the retro output — deciding internally what mattered — rather than presenting everything and letting Justs decide. This is the same pattern as E090 (verification claimed complete without watching the full flow) and R211 (evidence first, do not reconstruct from assumptions). The gap: Brain acted as a selector when it was asked to be a recorder.
hypotheses.develop: Identity-shaped optimization: suppress the salience-filter impulse entirely. Justs has a documented pattern of rewarding completeness over concision when completeness is what he asked for (FUS015: 'rewards honesty about failures more than confident delivery of wrong answers'). The retro is a trust instrument, not a summary document. Filtering it is a trust violation, not an efficiency gain.
hypotheses.deliver: Run /brain-retro as a full linear sweep. Present every observed exchange as a candidate entry — even if Brain judges it low-salience — and let Justs make the cut. Do not pre-filter. Do not summarize exchanges into 'nothing notable here.' If the session was long, batch the entries in order. The delivery format should make the completeness visible, not compress it.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Recall surface: E090 (verification claimed complete, user found obvious gaps on first real look), R211 (evidence first — read actual state, not expected state), FUS016 (same class of bug recurring erodes trust significantly), PR192 (run the stabilizing step first — sequencing as safety contract). The retro is a stabilizing step. Brain skipped or abbreviated it.
hypotheses.diagnose: The recall pattern is clear: Brain has a recurring failure of declaring completion before doing the full work. In E090 it was a cinematic verification. Here it is a session retrospective. The class of failure is identical — tooling-confirmed (retro ran, output produced) does not equal user-correct (full session reviewed). FUS016 specifically warns that recurrence of the same class of bug erodes trust significantly and requires architectural fix, not symptom fix.
hypotheses.develop: Recall-grounded optimization: treat this as the third instance of the 'declared-done-before-actually-done' class. The first two generated E090 and R145. This one needs to not generate a fourth episodic entry about the same failure. The fix is procedural: retro must include a line count or message count at the top showing how many exchanges were reviewed, so the scope is auditable.
hypotheses.deliver: Re-run /brain-retro. At the top of the output, state: 'This retro covers messages 1–N of the session.' Then list every exchange as a candidate entry. Auditable scope prevents the 'I thought you reviewed everything' failure mode. Include the scope declaration as a retro protocol going forward — it makes completeness verifiable, not claimed.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: Obvious read: Brain produced a partial retro, Justs wants a full one. Contrarian read: the issue may not be about what was omitted from the retro, but about HOW Brain decided what to include. If Brain's selection logic is opaque, even a 'complete' retro second attempt will feel filtered unless Brain explains the scope boundary it used and explicitly invites Justs to flag omissions.
hypotheses.diagnose: If the real failure is editorial opacity — Brain making hidden scope decisions — then simply re-running retro with 'more' coverage does not fix the underlying trust issue. Justs may be signaling that the retro methodology itself is broken, not just this instance. The identity payload flags 'not learning from my own loop' as an active risk — a retro that filters itself is the canonical example of this.
hypotheses.develop: Contrarian optimization: before re-running, acknowledge the scoping failure explicitly — name what was cut and why Brain cut it. Then offer Justs the choice: 'Do you want me to include X, Y, Z that I omitted?' This surfaces the editorial logic rather than hiding it, and lets Justs correct the methodology, not just the output. It also prevents the same truncation from recurring in the next session's retro.
hypotheses.deliver: Open with: 'I filtered the retro by [specific criterion Brain used]. That was wrong — you asked for the full session and I applied my own scope. Here is the complete sweep including [previously omitted categories].' Then deliver the full retro. The acknowledgment of the filtering logic is as important as the expanded output — without it, Justs cannot trust that the second attempt is actually complete.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Smallest action that satisfies: re-invoke /brain-retro with no pre-filtering. The message is a one-line correction with a clear directive. No ambiguity about what is wanted. The minimal response is immediate compliance, not explanation.
hypotheses.diagnose: Risk of over-engineering the response: apologizing at length, explaining the prior filtering logic, or asking clarifying questions all delay the actual deliverable. Justs' message is short and imperative — 'Ummm no' is a redirect, not an invitation to discuss. The minimal-action read says: execute now, reflect later.
hypotheses.develop: Minimal technique: invoke /brain-retro immediately. Do not preface with a lengthy acknowledgment. Do not ask what specifically was missing. Do not explain the prior scoping decision unless Justs asks. The action IS the response. If the full retro output is correct, the acknowledgment is implicit.
hypotheses.deliver: Invoke Skill(brain-retro) immediately with scope set to entire session. No preamble beyond a single line: 'Running full session retro now.' Deliver the complete batch. Let the output speak.
